generations.text:
My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you with a heavy heart, for the wind howls like a beast upon the cliffs and the sea roars as though it seeks to reclaim what it once held dear. The lamp burns with a steady glow, as it always has, but I fear the storm that brews upon the horizon may extinguish more than just the light.

Five years have passed since you left, and in that time, the world has not changed as much as I feared. The villagers still speak of you with a mixture of pity and disdain, and I have heard whispers that you have taken up with a man of questionable character. I do not know if this is true, but I have no wish to speak of it. You were always a headstrong child, and I pray that your spirit remains unbroken.

I have not written to you in this manner for many months, and I fear the silence has grown too long. I have not been able to find the words, not for want of trying, but for the fear that they would not be enough. You were once my greatest joy, and I have often wondered if I have been a poor father. I know I have been distant, and I know I have not been the man I should have been. But I ask you now, as the storm gathers strength and the sea rises in fury, to forgive me.

I have spent many nights watching the waves, hoping for a sign, a letter, a word from

The wind howls like a banshee, rattling the shutters and shaking the very bones of this old lighthouse. I've spent more than half a lifetime here, guiding ships through the dark, through the storms, through the silence that comes when the world forgets you. But tonight... tonight is different.

I've been waiting for this moment. Waiting for the storm to come, waiting for the silence to fall, waiting for the chance to say something I've never dared to say before.

You were just a child when you left, my little star. I watched you walk away, and I didn't stop you. I didn't even try to hold you back. I was too afraid to say the words that would have broken me. I was too afraid you'd leave anyway.

Five years. Five long, lonely years. I've kept the light burning, not just for the sailors, but for you. I've kept it burning so I wouldn't have to face the darkness alone.

But now... now the storm is upon us, and I feel it in my chest like a heartbeat. I can feel the end of the light, the end of the lighthouse, the end of me. And I can't let this be the last thing I say.

I've always been the keeper of the light, but tonight, I want to be the father who finally speaks. I want to tell you the truth, the whole truth, the truth I've carried in my heart

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these stone walls. The sea roars with a fury I have not heard in many years, and I fear the storm may be more than nature's wrath—it may be the end of me.

Five years have passed since you left, and yet the silence between us has grown heavier with each passing day. I have tried to write, many times, but the ink would always blur, and the words would not come. I thought perhaps you had forgotten me, or perhaps you had never truly loved me. But now, as I sit in the dim glow of the oil lamp, I find myself longing for your voice, for your presence, even if only in memory.

I have always been the keeper of this lighthouse, but tonight, I feel as though I am the keeper of my own soul. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the dawn. I have no one to call for help, no one to light the beacon in my place. You were always the light in my life, and now, I am lost in the darkness.

I know you must have heard of my illness, of the pain that has kept me from the duties I once held dear. I know you must have thought me weak, perhaps even unworthy of your care. But I have not forgotten the days when you would come to visit, when you would sit by the fire

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear I may not live to see it. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and in that time, I have come to understand the weight of silence, the ache of distance, and the bitterness of a father’s regret. I write to you now not only to apologize, but to explain—to tell you the truth of who I am, and why I have been so distant.

You were always my greatest joy, my light in the darkest hours. I remember the nights we spent by the fire, the way your laughter would fill the room, and the way your eyes would light up when you spoke of your dreams. But as you grew, so did the distance between us. You chose your path, and I chose mine. And though I tried to be there, I failed.

I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness. As a lighthouse keeper, I have spent my life standing guard over the sea, watching the horizon, and ensuring that no ship is lost to the dark. That same sense of responsibility has shaped me, and it has also kept me from the life I once dreamed of. I have loved you, Eleanor, but I have not always known how to show it. I have been afraid of what it would mean to let you go, and yet, in doing so, I have lost you.

Tonight, a storm is coming. I have seen the skies

**[Sealed with wax, bearing the crest of the Wexley Lighthouse, and a single tear-stained signature in faded ink]**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

*If you are reading this, then the storm has taken me. Or perhaps it has taken something else. Either way, I write now, with the wind howling like a banshee and the sea roiling as if it wishes to swallow the very earth. The lantern flickers, and the light has not yet gone out—though I fear it may soon. I pray you are safe, wherever you are, and that the sea has not claimed you.*

Five years, Eliza. Five long, cruel years. I have not seen you, nor heard from you. You sent no letters, no word, not even a single note. I expected it, in truth. You were always too proud, too stubborn, too much like your mother. But I did not expect the silence to last so long. I thought time would mend the wounds, that the sea would wash away the bitterness. But it has not. The sea is cruel, and so am I.

I have been alone here, in this lighthouse, for years. The wind howls through the cracks in the stone, and the waves crash against the rocks like the ghosts of those I have lost. I used to think that the lighthouse was a place of light and safety, but now I see it for what it

**[Sealed with a wax insignia of a storm-wracked lighthouse and a single, trembling heart.]**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not from age, but from the weight of the sea—its fury, its memory, its unspoken truths. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside, and the waves grow louder with each passing moment. I do not know if I shall see dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five years of silence, of letters unanswered, of nights spent staring into the abyss of the horizon, wondering if you would ever look back. I have kept the light burning for you, though I do not know if it was for you or for the ghosts of the ships that have been lost to the dark.

You were a child once, not the woman who once called me "father" and then turned her back. I remember the day you left. You stood at the edge of the cliff, the wind tearing at your coat, and you said nothing. I said nothing, too. I only watched as you walked away, and the sea swallowed your footprints before I could call you back.

I have not slept in days. The storm is coming—a great, black beast that even the sea fears. I have lit the lamp, though I do not know if it will be seen. I have lit it for the ships, for the sailors, for the lost. But

**My Dearest Eliza,**

It is with a heavy heart and a mind clouded by the weight of years that I take up my quill to write to you. The candlelight flickers like a dying star, casting long shadows upon the walls of this old lighthouse, and I find myself staring out at the sea, as I have done for countless nights, hoping that perhaps, just perhaps, you might one day come to see it with me.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I fear that the world has moved on without you, while I have remained, a solitary figure in this tower, watching the tides and the storms, as if they were the only companions I would ever have. I do not blame you for your silence, for I have been a poor father, a man of many words but few deeds, and a heart that has grown cold with time and regret.

The weather has turned most foul this evening, Eliza. The winds howl like the very devils of the sea, and the waves rise with a fury that I have not seen since the day you left. The sky is black as midnight, and the clouds churn like a cauldron of wrath, as if the heavens themselves are weeping. I have lit the lantern, as I always do, and the light pierces through the darkness, a beacon for the lost and the weary. I pray that it will guide the ships safely through these treacherous waters

Ah, my dear child, if you be reading this, then I fear the tempest has taken me from this mortal coil, and I shall not be here to see your face once more. Yet, I pray that you may find solace in these words, for they are the last I shall ever write.

You see, I am a lighthouse keeper, as you know, and though I have served this beacon for many a year, I have not seen you in five years, and I have not spoken to you in that time. I have not heard your voice, nor felt the warmth of your embrace, nor seen the light of your eyes. It is a cruel thing, I must say, to be a father and yet to be so far removed from his child. But I have been a fool, and I have let pride and silence keep me from you.

I have heard that you are married now, and that you have a son of your own. I pray that he is a good and noble lad, and that he has not taken after his mother in her ways. Though I have not seen him, I do hope that he is kind and gentle, for I have heard that he is a good-hearted boy.

Now, I must confess, I have not been a good father. I have not kept up with your letters, nor have I answered them. I have been too busy with the duties of this lighthouse, and I have let the sea and the wind take

**[Stained parchment, ink smudged from weather and age. The scent of salt and oil seeps from the paper. The letter is written in a shaky, uneven hand, the script jagged with emotion.]**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast in the night, the sea roaring with fury. The lighthouse stands, but I fear it may not stand much longer. The storm is coming, and I do not know if I will see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since I last saw your face, heard your voice. The days have stretched like the tides, endless, unrelenting. I have been alone here, with only the light to guide me and the sea to keep me company. I have kept the lamp burning, as I always have, for the sailors who rely on it. But now, I fear I may not be able to keep it burning much longer.

You were my light once, Eliza. You were the reason I chose this life, the reason I gave up the world beyond these cliffs. You were the child I held in my arms, the daughter I promised to raise with love and care. And yet, you left me. You left me behind, without a word, without a farewell.

I have tried to understand. I have searched for letters, for signs, for any clue that might explain why you walked

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

**Date:** April 3, 2025  
**Location:** The Cliffs of Marrow, North Atlantic

Dear Eliza,

It has been five long, bitter years since I last saw you, and I fear the time is drawing near when I may not see you again. Tonight, the sky is dark, the sea is wild, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. The storm is coming, and I am not sure I will make it through.

I am writing this letter with trembling hands, the light from the lighthouse flickering like a dying star. It is the last light on the coast, the beacon that guides ships through the storm and the calm alike. But tonight, it may be the last light of me as well.

You were young when you left, just twenty-two, with dreams of the city, of culture, of a life that wasn’t defined by the sea and the salt and the relentless winds. You called me a "sentimental, stubborn, and overly dramatic" man, and I suppose you were right. But I was not wrong in my love for this place, for this life, for the duty that binds me to the cliffs of Marrow.

I remember the last time we spoke. You told me that the sea was too loud, that the lighthouse was too lonely, that I had no soul. I told you that you had no soul

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a heavy heart, my hands trembling not from age alone, but from the weight of years unspoken and the sorrow of a father who has failed to be there for you. Five years, Eliza. Five long, dreadful years since I last saw you, and I fear the storm that now gathers on the horizon may not be the only thing to claim me.

I have not written to you, nor have I spoken, not because I do not care, but because I feared my words would not be enough. I feared they would not reach you, or worse, that they would not be the right ones. And so I let silence take the place of speech, and time take the place of love.

I have been here, Eliza, watching the sea, through every storm, through every calm, through every moment of quiet despair. I have been here, and I have endured. But I have not been whole. I have not been *you*.

I remember the day you left. The way you stood at the door, your eyes full of tears and your heart full of something I could not bear to see. I could not bear to say the words that would have broken me. I tried to say them, but they would not come. And so I let you go.

I have thought of you every single day since. I have spoken your name in the dark, when the wind howled and the waves crashed

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with regret, for I have not spoken to you in five years—five long, painful years. I know I have no right to write to you, to ask for your forgiveness or to beg for your understanding, but tonight, as the storm brews on the horizon, I find myself unable to hold my thoughts in.

You were only ten when I last saw you, and I can still see the way your eyes lit up at the sight of me, the way you ran to me with arms wide open, as if I had never been away. I was a fool to let pride and fear keep me from you. I told myself I was doing what was best, that I was protecting you from the harsh truths of this world. But in doing so, I lost you. And I have lost myself in the process.

The storm is coming, Eleanor. I can feel it in my bones. The wind howls like a beast, and the sea roars with a fury I've never known. I've been keeping the light burning all night, hoping it will guide the ships through the darkness. But I fear I may not make it through the night. I am old, and the sea is cruel. I have been keeping watch for so long that my strength is failing me.

I have no regrets

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I fear it may not be the case. Five years have passed since our last correspondence, and I have not written to you since the day you left. I have not spoken to you, nor have I tried. The silence between us has grown as thick as the fog that often shrouds the cliffs on which I reside, and I have come to believe that perhaps it is not a silence I can break, even if I wished to.

Tonight, a storm is gathering on the horizon, one of such fury and force that I have seen only in the most violent of tempests. The sea roars like a beast awakened from its slumber, and the wind howls as if it seeks to tear the very stones from the earth. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for many years, and I must confess that I feel a strange sense of unease. The light has been steady, but I wonder if it will be enough to guide the ships through the chaos that is to come.

I have always believed that the light of the lighthouse is a beacon not only for those at sea, but for those who have strayed from the path. I have kept this lamp burning through the darkest nights, through the coldest winters, through the loneliness that has settled in my bones. And yet, I have not felt the need to keep it for myself. Perhaps that is

I've been keeping the light burning for thirty years, child. Thirty years since I first saw you, a wobbly little thing with wide eyes and a too-big coat. You were just a child, and I was a man with a purpose, a duty, and a heart too hardened to know what it meant to love.

You left five years ago, and I've been alone ever since. No letters, no visits—just the wind and the waves and the endless night. I thought I'd be fine with it. Thought I'd grow used to the silence. But tonight... tonight the storm is coming, and I feel it in my bones. The wind is howling like a beast, the sea is rising, and I can't help but think of you.

I don't know if I'll make it through this. I don't know if the light will stay on or if the tower will fall. But I do know this: I've spent my life watching the sea, and I've never seen a storm like this. It's as if the sky itself is weeping.

You were always so impatient, always wanting to run away. You thought the world was too small for you, that you had to be somewhere else, doing something else. But I've been here all these years, standing guard over the rocks, the waves, and the darkness. And I've learned something, child—there's strength in stillness, in duty, in holding on when

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. Five long years have passed since the last time we spoke, and yet the silence between us has grown colder with each passing season. I have not forgotten the last words we exchanged, nor the way your eyes turned away from mine as if I were a stranger in a strange land.

The sea has been restless of late, and the winds have carried with them whispers of a storm unlike any we have known. The waves grow taller, the skies darken with an unnatural fury, and the lantern atop this tower flickers with an uncertain light. I fear for my life, but more than that, I fear for the legacy I have left behind.

You were always the light in my life, Eliza. Even when the world seemed to grow dark and cruel, you were the beacon that guided me through the storm. I often find myself gazing at the sea, wondering if you are still out there, if you have found peace, or if you still remember the man who once held you close.

I have not written to you because I feared the words might not be enough. I feared that even if I spoke, you would not listen. But now, as the storm gathers strength and the wind howls like a wounded beast, I feel the weight of my silence pressing upon me. I have spent too many years in regret, and I cannot bear to leave this world without one final message.

**Dear Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I suspect it may not. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not written to you since. I know you’ve moved on, and I have too. But tonight, as the storm gathers and the sea roars like a beast, I find myself compelled to reach out—not to change the past, but to make peace with it, if only in my own heart.

You were always my light, Eleanor. Even as a child, you brought me comfort in the darkest nights. I remember how you would sit by the window, watching the lighthouse beam sweep across the waves, and how you would whisper, “It’s guiding the ships home.” You were so young, yet you understood something I had not fully grasped until then. That light, that beam, is not just a tool—it is a promise, a hope, a guide. And I, in my pride and stubbornness, let that promise fade.

I know the things I said, the way I treated you, were harsh. I was a man of the sea, and the sea has a way of making us cruel. I believed I was protecting you, that the world was too dangerous, too full of pain, and that I had to shield you from it. But in doing so, I became the very thing I feared—cold, distant, and unyielding. I let fear harden my heart, and in

**[Sealed with wax, bearing the crest of the *Lighthouse of Blackmoor Bay*]**

**To My Dearest Elara,**

**If you are reading this, then the sea has claimed me, and the storm has swallowed the lighthouse whole.** I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old stone, and the sea roars as though it seeks to reclaim what was once its own. The lantern has long since died, and the light that once guided sailors home now flickers only in the dark of my soul.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters unanswered, of my heart laid bare upon the cold, salted stones of this lonely keep. I have watched the sun rise and set, the tides ebb and flow, and the stars blink like distant eyes above. And still, you have not come.

I do not know if you still remember me, Elara. I fear that time has worn the name of your father from your memory, like the tide erodes the shore. But if you do remember, I beg you to know this: I have never ceased to love you. Not once. Not in the years you vanished, nor in the years I have stood alone, watching the world go on without me.

I have been a fool, I know. A man who let pride and fear keep him from reaching for the only thing that

*The letter is written in a rough, uneven hand, with ink smudged by the sea breeze and the weight of years. It is folded with care, as if it might crumble if handled too roughly.*

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the window, the wind howling like a beast outside, the sea roiling with a fury that makes my bones ache. The lantern burns low, its light flickering like a soul in torment. I’ve been keeping it lit all night, not just for the ships, but for *you*—for the ghost of the child I once knew.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left. Five years since I stopped speaking your name aloud, afraid it might summon you back. I’ve told myself you were better off without me, that the world was too cruel for a girl with your fire. But tonight… tonight, the storm has made me see the truth.

You were never the one who ran. You were the one who stayed behind, clinging to a dream I could never understand. I was a man of stone and salt, while you were a flame that would not be contained. And I, in my pride, let you go.

Ah, my dear Eliza,

If you are reading these words, then I must have survived the night, though I fear I may not have the strength to write much more. The tempest rages with a fury that seems to echo the storm in my soul, and I find myself compelled to speak, even if only to the winds that howl through the lantern room.

Five years, my child. Five years since I last saw your face, or heard your voice. Time, that cruel and patient adversary, has dragged its weary heels through the years, and I find myself more alone now than ever before. Not in the sense of solitude, for I have the company of the sea and the ever-watchful stars, but in the absence of your presence, which I once found so comforting in these lonely hours.

I have often wondered if you still remember the lighthouse, or if the world has moved on without you. I have not written to you for so long, not out of neglect, but because I feared the words I would speak would be but echoes of a man who has long since lost his way. Yet now, as the storm gathers strength, I feel the need to speak, to pour out my heart, as one might pour out a dram of brandy on a cold night.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

**I hope this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I fear that in these days of late, I have not been in a position to send you much in the way of letters.** I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years now, and though I have seen many a storm, none have been as fierce as the one that threatens us tonight. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea rages with a fury that would make even the most seasoned sailor tremble. I fear that I may not see the dawn, and so I have taken it upon myself to write this letter to you, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I would not have it said that I neglected my duty as a father.

**I know that our last meeting was not a happy one.** You were but a young lady, and I, a grizzled old man with more salt in my veins than the sea itself. You were quick to take offense at my manner of speaking, and I, in my old age, was not quick to mend fences. I must say, I have been sorely remiss in not writing to you more often, and I must ask your forgiveness for the pain I may have caused you. I have been a poor father, and I am but a humble lighthouse keeper, yet I would have done more to keep our bond unbroken.

**To My Beloved Daughter,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse, the sea roaring as if it would swallow the very earth beneath me. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see the dawn. Yet, I must write, for I cannot bear the thought of leaving this letter unspoken, and you, my child, without the truth.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of longing, of watching the waves crash against the cliffs as I did every night, every morning, every evening. I have kept the light burning, not just for the ships that pass, but for you. I have watched the stars, the moon, the sun, and in them I have seen your face, your voice, your laughter. I have dreamed of you, and in those dreams, I have been both father and son, both the one who sent you away and the one who begged you not to go.

I know what you think of me. You think I was cruel, that I was blind, that I chose duty over love. But I was not blind. I saw the fire in your eyes, the hunger in your soul, the need to be more than what I could offer. You were not the child I raised. **From the Lighthouse at the End of the World**  
*April 5th, 1892*  
*Storm on the Horizon*  

My Dearest Eliza,  

It has been five long years since I last saw you, and five years since I last spoke to you, not in the way of a father to a daughter, but in the way of a stranger passing on the street. Time has been a cruel and unrelenting force, but tonight, as the storm gathers strength over the sea, I find myself writing this letter with a heart both heavy and full of longing.

You must not be surprised to learn that I am still alive. I was not certain, not until the wind began to howl like a wounded beast and the waves began to rise like a vengeful tide. The lighthouse, once a steadfast sentinel of the sea, now stands on the edge of destruction, and I, its keeper, may not be the one to see it through.

You know, of course, that I have always been a man of reason and duty. I have spent my life in service to the light, to the sea, and to the people who depend on it. I have never sought glory, nor have I ever sought recognition. But I have always sought something more than the quiet life of a lighthouse keeper—something more than the routine of oil lamps and fog signals. I have always sought *you*.

I know the pain of

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you far from where you ought to be. Five long years have passed since I last laid eyes upon you, and I find myself writing now with a heart heavy with regret and a mind clouded by the weight of time.

I have often thought of you, Eliza. Not in the way a man might think of a son or daughter when he is alone in the lighthouse, but with a feeling that is deeper, more bitter, more sorrowful. You were always the light in my life, and now I fear I have become the shadow.

I know you must think me a cruel man, one who turned his back on his own flesh. But I beg you, do not let that thought stand. I have done what I believed was right, for your own good, and for the good of the lighthouse, and for the people who depend upon it. You do not know the burden I carry, the responsibility that weighs on me night after night, year after year. I have given everything to this duty, and in doing so, I have lost myself.

I have not written to you because I feared my words would not be enough. I feared I would say the wrong thing, or worse, that I would say nothing at all. And now, as the storm gathers, I find that I have nothing left to fear but the night.

You know I have always

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. I’ve been writing it for hours, my hands shaking with the weight of words I’ve long kept locked away. Five years have passed since you left, and I’ve not spoken a single word to you since. The silence has been deafening, and yet, I have kept it—because I thought it was the only way to protect you.

Tonight, a storm is coming. Not just any storm, but a howling tempest that has already begun to tear at the cliffs and the sea. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves rise like mountains, crashing against the rocks with a fury that seems almost divine. I have never felt such fear in my years as a lighthouse keeper. I have stood through many storms, but this one… this one feels different. It feels like it may take more than just the lighthouse.

I’ve been thinking a lot about you lately. About the last time we spoke, the way you looked at me, not with anger, but with something deeper—something I can’t quite name. I know I failed you. I know I let you down. I should have been there for you when you needed me most. I should have listened, not just to your words, but to your heart. I should have known that you were not just upset, but hurt. And I didn’t do

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with the ink trembling in my hand, for the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet the weight of silence has grown heavier than the stones that guard this lighthouse. I have kept the lamp burning through every storm, every night, every weary hour, and I have kept the light steady for all who sail these waters. But tonight, I fear the sea may be too fierce, and I may not be able to keep it alight.

You were a child when you left, and I thought the sea would take you. I have watched the waves for years, hoping to see your return, but the horizon has only ever held the same endless blue. I have tried to forgive you, as the sea forgives the rocks, but I have found it hard to let go. You were my only child, and I have always been a man of duty, of duty to the light, to the sea, to the people who depend on my watch. But I have also been a man of love, and I have loved you more than I have ever loved the sea.

I have not written to you because I feared my words would be unkind, or worse, unworthy. I have spent years trying to be the kind of father you deserved, and I have failed. I know now that I was wrong to let

I’ve spent years standing here, alone, with only the wind and the sea to keep me company. The lighthouse is my home, my refuge, my prison. But tonight—tonight is different. The sky is dark, the waves are rising, and the wind howls like it knows something I don’t. Something I can’t explain.

Five years. Five long, silent years. I’ve tried to forget you, to move on, to pretend that you never left. But I can’t. Not when the storm is coming and I can’t even tell if I’ll live to see dawn.

You were my light once, my little star. I used to watch you from the tower, your laughter echoing down the coast, your voice like the sea’s gentle song. But then you left, and the sea turned against me. It took you, and it took my heart with it.

I’ve been so angry. So bitter. I thought if I stayed here, far from the world, I could forget the pain. But the pain never left. It lingers in the cold wind, in the salt on my skin, in the silence that follows every storm.

And now, as the sky cracks open and the waves crash against the rocks, I feel something I haven’t felt in years. I feel… hope. Not for the storm, not for the sea, but for you. For the chance to tell you the truth.

I’ve been wrong, you know.

**Dear Eliza,**

**To My Dearest Elara,**
